questions.question: What should be assessed before approaching a patient in behavioral crisis?
options: A. Scene safety from a distance — weapons, exits, patient affect
B. Nothing — approach immediately to begin assessment
C. Only the chief complaint reported by dispatch
D. The patient's insurance status
questions.rationale: Scene safety assessment from a distance precedes patient contact in behavioral emergencies, given the elevated risk of unpredictable violence.

questions.question: What should happen when a credible safety concern is identified on a behavioral call?
options: A. Proceed with patient contact regardless
B. Involve law enforcement per local protocol before approaching
C. Ignore the concern if the call seems urgent
D. Wait indefinitely with no action
questions.rationale: Credible safety concerns should prompt law enforcement involvement per local protocol before EMS approaches the patient.

questions.question: What does SEA-3 stand for?
options: A. Safety, Emotional state, Appearance, Activity, Speech
B. Scene, Exam, Assessment, Airway, Attitude
C. Signs, Events, Allergies, Age, Activity
D. Safety, Escalation, Anxiety, Agitation, Awareness
questions.rationale: SEA-3 stands for Safety, Emotional state, Appearance, Activity, and Speech.

questions.question: What is assessed under 'Appearance' in the SEA-3 framework?
options: A. Grooming, hygiene, and dress as clues to functioning
B. Blood pressure and heart rate
C. Pupil size
D. Skin color only
questions.rationale: Appearance in SEA-3 refers to grooming, hygiene, and dress, which can indicate a patient's level of functioning.

questions.question: What is assessed under 'Activity' in the SEA-3 framework?
options: A. Psychomotor activity — agitation, retardation, restlessness
B. Recent physical exercise history
C. Employment status
D. Medication compliance
questions.rationale: Activity in SEA-3 refers to psychomotor activity level, such as agitation or retardation.

questions.question: What type of questioning is preferred during de-escalation?
options: A. Rapid-fire closed questions
B. Open-ended questions
C. No questions at all
D. Only yes/no questions
questions.rationale: Open-ended questions are preferred during de-escalation, as they invite the patient to share more and feel less interrogated.

questions.question: Should a provider argue with or directly challenge a patient's delusional content?
options: A. Yes, to correct the misperception
B. No — avoid arguing with or directly challenging delusional content
C. Only if the delusion seems dangerous
D. Only during transport
questions.rationale: De-escalation avoids arguing with or challenging delusional content directly, as this tends to increase agitation rather than help.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of field de-escalation?
options: A. To resolve the underlying psychiatric condition
B. To reduce the patient's perceived threat level and build cooperation for safe assessment and transport
C. To convince the patient they are wrong
D. To sedate the patient immediately
questions.rationale: De-escalation aims to reduce perceived threat and build cooperation, not to treat or resolve the psychiatric condition itself in the field.

questions.question: When is physical restraint an appropriate first response in a behavioral emergency?
options: A. It is always the first response for agitated patients
B. It is a last resort, used only after de-escalation fails and the patient poses immediate danger
C. It should be used for every behavioral call to prevent injury
D. It is never appropriate under any circumstances
questions.rationale: Restraint is reserved for situations where de-escalation has failed and immediate danger exists — not a first response.

questions.question: What is abandonment in the EMS context?
options: A. Refusing to transport a patient who declines care
B. Terminating patient care without ensuring an equal or higher level of care continues
C. Documenting a patient's refusal of care
D. Calling for additional resources on scene
questions.rationale: Abandonment specifically means stopping care without transferring the patient to an equal or higher level of care.

questions.question: What distinction matters when a patient refuses care?
options: A. Whether the patient competently refuses (with informed-refusal documentation) versus lacking capacity to refuse
B. Only whether the patient is polite about refusing
C. Whether the refusal happens indoors or outdoors
D. There is no meaningful distinction to make
questions.rationale: Distinguishing a competent, informed refusal from a refusal by a patient lacking capacity is central to avoiding both inappropriate care and abandonment.

questions.question: A patient shows disorganized speech, poor grooming, and marked psychomotor agitation together. How should this combination be interpreted?
options: A. As no more significant than any single finding alone
B. As a higher-risk overall picture than any one finding in isolation
C. As evidence the patient is exaggerating symptoms
D. As irrelevant to behavioral risk assessment
questions.rationale: The SEA-3 framework's value comes from viewing findings together — multiple concerning findings combined paint a higher-risk picture than any single element alone.

questions.question: Why is scene safety assessment from a distance emphasized in behavioral emergencies specifically?
options: A. Because agitated, psychotic, or intoxicated patients can become unpredictably violent, and provider safety is a prerequisite for helping the patient
B. Because behavioral patients are never dangerous
C. Because it is only a formality with no real safety benefit
D. Because it delays care unnecessarily and should be skipped when possible
questions.rationale: Behavioral emergencies carry elevated unpredictability risk; assessing safety before approach protects providers so they can actually deliver care.

questions.question: What non-verbal de-escalation techniques are recommended?
options: A. Maintaining a non-threatening posture and appropriate distance, avoiding sudden movements
B. Standing very close and making direct, prolonged eye contact
C. Crossing arms and standing directly in front of the patient
D. Moving quickly to physically guide the patient
questions.rationale: Non-threatening posture, appropriate distance, and avoiding sudden movements are core non-verbal de-escalation techniques.

questions.question: A behavioral-emergency patient competently and clearly refuses transport after a thorough capacity assessment and informed-refusal discussion. What should the provider do?
options: A. Physically restrain and transport against the patient's will regardless of capacity
B. Document the informed refusal per protocol and provide appropriate resources/follow-up guidance, respecting the patient's autonomy
C. Abandon the patient without any documentation
D. Ignore the refusal and repeat the same questions indefinitely
questions.rationale: A competent, informed refusal should be respected and properly documented — this is different from abandonment, which involves terminating care inappropriately without ensuring continuity for a patient who needs it.
